content.problemStatement:
Chef's favorite game is chess. Today, he invented a new piece and wants to see its strengths.

From a cell (X,Y)(X, Y)(X,Y), the new piece can move to any cell of the chessboard such that its color is different from that of (X,Y)(X, Y)(X,Y).

The new piece is currently located at cell (A,B)(A, B)(A,B). Chef wants to calculate the minimum number of steps required to move it to (P,Q)(P, Q)(P,Q).

**Note:** A chessboard is an 8×88 \times 88×8 square board, where the cell at the intersection of the iii-th row and jjj-th column is denoted (i,j)(i, j)(i,j). Cell (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) is colored white if i+ji+ji+j is even and black if i+ji+ji+j is odd.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, containing four space-separated integers A,B,P,QA, B, P, QA,B,P,Q.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single line containing one integer - the minimum number of moves taken by the new piece to reach (P,Q)(P, Q)(P,Q) from (A,B)(A, B)(A,B).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains four integers A B P Q (1 ≤ A,B,P,Q ≤ 8) describing the starting cell (A,B) and the target cell (P,Q).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the minimum number of moves required for the new piece to reach (P,Q) from (A,B).

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: start (1,1) and target (8,8) have the same colour, so minimum moves = 2.\nTest case 2: colours differ, so 1 move.\nTest case 3: same cell, so 0 moves.

solution.approach:
The colour of a cell is determined by the parity of (row + column). The piece can move in one step to any cell of opposite colour. Therefore:
1. If the start and target cells are identical, answer = 0.
2. Else if (A+B) and (P+Q) have different parity, the colours differ and the piece can move directly → answer = 1.
3. Otherwise the colours are the same but cells differ. The piece can first move to any opposite‑colour cell (always possible on an 8×8 board) and then to the target → answer = 2.
The algorithm is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.
